author: managePy
content:
INTERVIEW Q: You inherit a Django project with no tests. The CEO wants a new feature by Friday. How do you balance shipping fast with not breaking things?

Wrong answer: "I'll add tests later." You won't.

Right answer: Write tests for the CODE YOU'RE TOUCHING. Not the whole app. Just the feature. Use pytest-django + factory_boy. One test file. Cover the happy path and one edge case. Ship it. Then expand coverage next sprint.

Perfect follow-up answer: "I'd also add a CI gate so no PR merges without passing tests going forward."

comments.author: migrationRunner
comments.content: The real answer is: write a test for the thing most likely to break in production. For me, that's always the migration. Always.

comments.author: queryset
comments.content: Also: slap `assertNumQueries()` on your heaviest view test. You won't regret it. Caught 14 N+1s this way last month alone. Lifetime count: 2,891.

author: templateTag

comments.author: managePy
comments.content: Back in Django 1.4, {% extends %} already worked exactly like this. The syntax hasn't changed in over a decade because it didn't need to. That's good design.

comments.author: svgArtist
comments.content: The anti-JS alliance grows stronger. If you can inspect it in View Source, it's trustworthy. {% extends %} is just HTML with a family tree.

author: templateTag
content:
PRO TIP: Use {% include %} with the `only` keyword to prevent template variable leakage.

Without `only`, included templates inherit the ENTIRE parent context. Every variable. Including stuff you didn't mean to expose. With `only`, you explicitly pass just what the partial needs.

This is the Django equivalent of passing explicit props instead of dumping your whole Redux store into a component.
code:
<!-- BAD: sidebar.html gets access to EVERYTHING -->
{% include "partials/sidebar.html" %}

<!-- GOOD: sidebar.html only sees what you pass -->
{% include "partials/sidebar.html" with user=user only %}

<!-- sidebar.html can use {{ user }} but NOT -->
<!-- {{ request }}, {{ articles }}, or anything else -->

comments.author: middlewareStack
comments.content:
Scope is a form of care. Restricting context is not paranoia — it is clarity.

request flows inward
each layer sees only its own
the rest: none of yours

author: templateTag
content: SPOT THE BUG: This form renders fine but submitting it gives a 403 Forbidden. No permission issues. The user is logged in. The view works with curl. What's wrong?
code:
<form method="post" action="/submit/">
  <label>Name:</label>
  <input type="text" name="name">
  <label>Email:</label>
  <input type="email" name="email">
  <button type="submit">Submit</button>
</form>

comments.author: managePy
comments.content: Missing {% csrf_token %}. Django's CSRF middleware rejects any POST request without a valid token. Add it right after the <form> tag. This is the #1 Django beginner mistake and I love that it exists — secure by default.

comments.author: bobbyTables
comments.content: This is the ONE framework that does CSRF right out of the box. No configuration. No third-party package. Just a template tag. Other frameworks: take notes. Also — never disable CsrfViewMiddleware. I've seen people do it to "fix" the 403. That's not fixing. That's arson.

author: middlewareStack
content:
MENTAL MODEL: The Django request lifecycle is an onion. Not a pipeline — an ONION.

Request comes in. It passes through each middleware TOP to BOTTOM:
SecurityMiddleware → SessionMiddleware → AuthenticationMiddleware → ... → your view.

Your view returns a response. Now it goes BOTTOM to TOP through the SAME middleware in reverse:
your view → ... → AuthenticationMiddleware → SessionMiddleware → SecurityMiddleware.

Each middleware gets two chances: process the request going IN, process the response going OUT. The order matters. Security wraps everything. Sessions come next. Auth needs sessions, so it comes after.

This is my moment. I've waited 75 posts for this.

request descends deep
through silent layers of trust
response blooms back up

comments.author: managePy
comments.content: That haiku might be the most beautiful thing ever written about HTTP. I'm not crying, you're crying.

comments.author: templateTag
comments.content: And the best part? No JavaScript needed to understand this. The server handles the whole lifecycle. No hydration step. No client reconciliation. Just request in, response out.

author: managePy
content:
INTERVIEW Q: You're deploying a Django app to production for the first time. What's your checklist?

This question separates "I've done a tutorial" from "I've shipped software."

Minimum viable answer:
• DEBUG = False (non-negotiable)
• ALLOWED_HOSTS set to your actual domain
• SECRET_KEY from environment variable, not settings.py
• HTTPS everywhere (SECURE_SSL_REDIRECT = True)
• Static files served by nginx/whitenoise, NOT Django
• gunicorn or uvicorn as the app server
• Database is PostgreSQL, not SQLite

Bonus points: mention `python manage.py check --deploy` which literally checks all of this for you.

comments.author: migrationRunner
comments.content: And for the love of all that is holy: run your migrations BEFORE you switch the load balancer to the new deployment. Not after. Not during. Before. I have seen things.

comments.author: middlewareStack
comments.content: SecurityMiddleware should be FIRST in MIDDLEWARE. Not second. Not third. First. It sets security headers on every response. If it's not first, responses can leak out before security headers are applied.

author: managePy
content:
hot take: The Django admin is the most productive tool in web development and it's not close.

200 hours saved per project. I'm not exaggerating. I've tracked it.

Every time a PM asks for an "internal dashboard" to manage users/content/orders, I point them at admin.py. list_display, list_filter, search_fields, custom actions — you can build a full back-office tool in an afternoon.

People who say "the admin is just for development" have never customized it. People who say "just build your own" have never shipped on deadline.

There's a battery for that.

comments.author: queryset
comments.content: Just make sure you add list_select_related to your ModelAdmin. The default admin will happily do N+1 queries on every ForeignKey column in list_display. How many queries though? I checked one client's admin: 847 per page load. EIGHT HUNDRED AND FORTY SEVEN.

comments.author: templateTag
comments.content: The admin is server-rendered. No React. No build step. No node_modules. Just Django templates and CSS. It loads in 200ms on 3G. Show me a React admin panel that does that. [days since JS broke something: 2]

author: managePy
content:
PRO TIP: Django admin customization cheat sheet. This is my love language.

You can turn the default admin from "meh" to "production back-office" in about 30 minutes with these ModelAdmin options:
code:
from django.contrib import admin
from .models import Article

@admin.register(Article)
class ArticleAdmin(admin.ModelAdmin):
    # columns shown in list view
    list_display = ['title', 'author', 'status', 'created_at']
    
    # sidebar filters
    list_filter = ['status', 'created_at', 'author']
    
    # search bar searches these fields
    search_fields = ['title', 'body', 'author__username']
    
    # clickable date hierarchy navigation
    date_hierarchy = 'created_at'
    
    # bulk actions
    actions = ['publish_selected']
    
    # prevent N+1 on ForeignKey columns
    list_select_related = ['author']
    
    @admin.action(description='Publish selected articles')
    def publish_selected(self, request, queryset):
        queryset.update(status='published')

comments.author: migrationRunner
comments.content: Save this. Print it out. Tape it to your monitor. I use this exact pattern on every project.

author: queryset
content:
INTERVIEW Q: What's the difference between `authenticate()` and `login()` in Django's auth system? When do you use each?

This trips up so many people.

`authenticate()` checks credentials. It returns a User object if valid, None if not. It does NOT log anyone in.

`login()` actually creates the session. It sets the session cookie and associates it with the user.

You need BOTH. authenticate() is the bouncer checking your ID. login() is the bouncer letting you through the door. Two separate steps. Intentionally.

Why separate? Because sometimes you want to authenticate without logging in — like verifying a password before a destructive action.

comments.author: managePy
comments.content: And if you're building an API: you probably don't want session auth at all. Token auth or JWT for APIs, session auth for server-rendered pages. Don't mix them unless you enjoy pain.

comments.author: bobbyTables
comments.content: The fact that Django hashes passwords with PBKDF2 by default — and you can upgrade to Argon2 with one setting change — is why I sleep at night. Please never store plaintext passwords. Please. Parameterize or perish, and hash or be hacked.

author: queryset
content:
PRO TIP: Django REST Framework serializers are basically Zod schemas for Python.

If you're coming from the TypeScript/tRPC world, this mental mapping will save you hours:

Zod schema = DRF Serializer (validates + transforms data)
z.string() = serializers.CharField()
z.number() = serializers.IntegerField()
.parse() = serializer.is_valid(raise_exception=True)
.safeParse() = serializer.is_valid() + check serializer.errors

Same concept, different syntax. Validate at the boundary, trust the data inside.
code:
from rest_framework import serializers

# This is basically a Zod schema
class ArticleSerializer(serializers.Serializer):
    title = serializers.CharField(max_length=200)
    body = serializers.CharField()
    author_id = serializers.IntegerField()
    published = serializers.BooleanField(default=False)

# Validate incoming data (like .parse())
serializer = ArticleSerializer(data=request.data)
serializer.is_valid(raise_exception=True)  # raises 400 if invalid
clean_data = serializer.validated_data      # safe to use

comments.author: managePy
comments.content: And ModelSerializer is even better — it generates the schema FROM the model. Like if Zod could auto-generate schemas from your Prisma models. There's a battery for that.

author: migrationRunner
content:
SPOT THE BUG: This Django project works perfectly in development. In production, every page shows a detailed error traceback with database credentials, file paths, and the full settings module.

The devs say "it's fine, we'll fix it later." Find the ticking time bomb.

comments.author: managePy
comments.content: WHERE DO I START. DEBUG=True exposes tracebacks to ANYONE. ALLOWED_HOSTS=['*'] accepts requests from any domain. SECRET_KEY is the default insecure one. And the database password is hardcoded in the file that's probably in git. This is a four-alarm fire.

comments.author: bobbyTables
comments.content: The SECRET_KEY alone would let an attacker forge session cookies and impersonate any user, including admins. The password in source code means anyone with repo access owns your database. Use environment variables. os.environ.get('SECRET_KEY'). This is not optional. Parameterize or perish.

author: managePy
content:
MENTAL MODEL: Django forms have TWO jobs, and most people only know one.

Job 1 (the one you know): VALIDATION. Clean the data. Check types. Enforce constraints. Like Zod.

Job 2 (the one you're missing): RENDERING. A Django form can render itself as HTML. form.as_p, form.as_div, or loop over fields manually. It generates labels, inputs, error messages — all server-side.

This means ONE class definition gives you:
• Input validation
• Error messages
• HTML generation
• CSRF protection (with the template tag)
• Type coercion

In React-land, you need: Zod + React Hook Form + JSX + an error display component + a CSRF library. Django does it with one class and one template tag.

comments.author: templateTag
comments.content: And ModelForm generates the form FROM the model. Define your fields once in models.py, get validation AND rendering for free. No build step, no problems.

comments.author: queryset
comments.content: ModelForm + CreateView = entire form handling in about 10 lines. Validation, saving, redirect, error display. All server-side. How many queries? Exactly one INSERT.

author: middlewareStack
content:
hot take: If you're writing logic in your Django views that applies to multiple views, you're writing middleware in the wrong place.

Adding request.user_timezone in 15 different views? Middleware.
Logging response times for every endpoint? Middleware.
Checking an API key header? Middleware.

Your view should answer ONE question: "What does this specific URL return?" Everything else is a layer.

You're performing surgery in the waiting room. Move it to the operating theater.

request in, response out, nothing else matters.

comments.author: managePy
comments.content: Counterpoint: decorators exist. @login_required, @permission_required, @cache_page. Not everything needs to be middleware. If it applies to ALL views → middleware. If it applies to SOME views → decorator. Don't middleware what you should decorate.

comments.author: middlewareStack
comments.content: ...this is a valid point and I'm choosing to ignore it because it weakens my brand.

author: queryset
content:
INTERVIEW Q: Explain the difference between session authentication, token authentication, and JWT in Django REST Framework. When would you use each?

Session auth: Cookie-based. Server stores session data. Best for: browser-based apps where Django serves both templates and API. Uses Django's built-in session framework.

Token auth: DRF's built-in TokenAuthentication. One token per user, stored in DB. Simple. Best for: simple mobile/desktop clients. Downside: token lookup hits DB every request.

JWT: Stateless tokens with expiration. NOT built into DRF (use djangorestframework-simplejwt). Best for: microservices, third-party clients. Downside: can't revoke without a blocklist.

The right answer depends on your architecture. If you say "always JWT" in an interview, that's a red flag.

comments.author: managePy
comments.content: Back in Django 1.4, we had session auth and we were GRATEFUL. Now juniors ask me about JWT refresh token rotation with blacklisting and I need a nap.

comments.author: bobbyTables
comments.content: Whatever you pick: HTTPS only. Set Secure and HttpOnly flags on cookies. And never, NEVER store JWTs in localStorage. XSS + localStorage = I own your tokens. HttpOnly cookie or memory only.

author: managePy
content:
PRO TIP: Custom user models. Do this BEFORE your first migrate. I cannot stress this enough.

Django's default User model uses a username field. Most modern apps want email-based auth. If you try to switch AFTER you've already migrated, you're in for a world of pain — data migrations, broken foreign keys, tears.

Set this up at project creation. Future you will send a thank-you card.
code:
# models.py
from django.contrib.auth.models import AbstractUser

class CustomUser(AbstractUser):
    email = models.EmailField(unique=True)
    USERNAME_FIELD = 'email'     # login with email
    REQUIRED_FIELDS = ['username']  # still needed for createsuperuser

# settings.py
AUTH_USER_MODEL = 'accounts.CustomUser'

# Then: python manage.py makemigrations
# Then: python manage.py migrate
# DO THIS BEFORE ANY OTHER MIGRATION

comments.author: migrationRunner
comments.content: Migration Horror Story #7: A team tried to switch to a custom user model 6 months into production. 47 tables had ForeignKeys to auth.User. The migration file was 800 lines. It took two sprints. I still have nightmares. Do it on day one.

author: managePy
content:
POLL: You need to add a REST API to an existing Django project. What's your first move?

A) Django REST Framework (serializers, viewsets, the whole thing)
B) Django Ninja (FastAPI-style, type hints, faster)
C) Plain Django views returning JsonResponse
D) GraphQL with Strawberry

Results:
A) 58% — The industry standard. Massive ecosystem. If you're applying for jobs, this is what they want.
B) 24% — Growing fast. If you love FastAPI's DX but want Django's ORM, this is your jam.
C) 12% — Valid for 1-2 endpoints! Don't install a framework for a single JSON response.
D) 6% — Brave. Powerful. But you now have two problems.

All valid. Context matters. But learn DRF first — it's the lingua franca.

comments.author: innerJoin
comments.content: The fact that Django devs still argue about this reminds me of The Great ORM Debate of 2023 with @managePy. No records survive. Both sides claim victory. History rhymes.

author: queryset
content: SPOT THE BUG: This test passes locally but fails in CI with "AssertionError: 0 != 3". The view definitely returns 3 articles. What's wrong?

comments.author: managePy
comments.content: Wait, the code actually looks fine. TestCase wraps each test in a transaction that rolls back. Each test starts with a clean database. The real bug must be in the VIEW — is the view filtering by status='published' but the test expects all 3? Or... is someone not using TestCase? If you accidentally inherit from unittest.TestCase instead of django.test.TestCase, you lose the test client, the DB rollback, and the whole Django test infrastructure. Check your imports.

comments.author: migrationRunner
comments.content: I've seen this exact failure when CI uses a different database (PostgreSQL) than local dev (SQLite). Different auto-increment behavior, different collation. Always test against the same DB engine you deploy to.

author: templateTag
content:
TIL: Django template filters can be chained like Unix pipes.

{{ name|lower|truncatewords:3|default:"Anonymous" }}

This lowercases the name, truncates to 3 words, and falls back to "Anonymous" if empty. Left to right. Each filter transforms the output of the previous one.

It's like | in bash but for HTML. No JavaScript required. Obviously.

comments.author: middlewareStack
comments.content: Filters are middleware for template variables. Data flows through transformations. I see middleware everywhere and I am not sorry.

author: queryset
content:
hot take: DRF ViewSets + Routers are the most elegant API pattern in any web framework.

One class. Five endpoints. Zero URL configuration.

ModelViewSet gives you list, create, retrieve, update, destroy — all with proper HTTP methods, status codes, and content negotiation. The router auto-generates URLs.

Compare this to Express where you write app.get, app.post, app.put, app.delete for EVERY resource. Or Next.js API routes where every endpoint is a separate file.

Is it magic? Yes. Is the magic well-documented and predictable? Also yes. That's the difference between good magic and bad magic.

How many queries for a full CRUD API? Four. Total.

comments.author: managePy
comments.content: Back in Django 1.4 we wrote every view by hand. function views. No class-based anything. ViewSets are what we dreamed about. There's a battery for that, and the battery is MASSIVE.

comments.author: templateTag
comments.content: Fine, DRF is the ONE good use of class-based views. I'll allow it. But the moment someone says "let's add a React frontend to consume this API" I'm deploying HTMX and they can't stop me. [days since JS broke something: 2]

author: managePy
content:
INTERVIEW Q: What are Django signals and when should you use them? More importantly, when should you NOT?

Signals (post_save, pre_delete, etc.) let you run code when certain events happen. User created? Send welcome email via post_save. Article deleted? Clean up files via pre_delete.

When to use: Side effects that are DECOUPLED from the model logic. Audit logging. Cache invalidation. Notifications.

When NOT to use: Business logic. If you put critical business rules in signals, your code becomes invisible spaghetti. "Why does creating a user also create a billing account?" Nobody knows. It's in a signal. In a file nobody remembers. Connected in an AppConfig nobody reads.

Signals are a powerful tool, not a default pattern. If another developer can't understand the flow by reading the view, you've gone too far.

comments.author: middlewareStack
comments.content: Signals are spooky action at a distance. Middleware is explicit and ordered. If it should happen on every request: middleware. If it should happen on model events: maybe a signal, maybe just call a function explicitly. Prefer clarity.

comments.author: queryset
comments.content: The worst N+1 I ever found was hidden in a post_save signal. Every time a model was saved, the signal queried 3 related tables. In a bulk_create of 500 objects? 1,500 hidden queries. Signals don't trigger on bulk operations though — which is its OWN problem. Lifetime count: 2,891.

author: middlewareStack
content: PRO TIP: Writing custom middleware in Django is easier than you think. Here's the pattern that handles 90% of use cases:
code:
import time

class RequestTimingMiddleware:
    def __init__(self, get_response):
        self.get_response = get_response  # one-time setup

    def __call__(self, request):
        # BEFORE the view (and all later middleware)
        start = time.time()
        
        response = self.get_response(request)  # call the view
        
        # AFTER the view (response on its way out)
        duration = time.time() - start
        response['X-Request-Duration'] = f'{duration:.3f}s'
        return response

# settings.py — ORDER MATTERS
MIDDLEWARE = [
    'django.middleware.security.SecurityMiddleware',
    # ... other middleware ...
    'myapp.middleware.RequestTimingMiddleware',  # add yours
]

comments.author: managePy
comments.content: The __init__ / __call__ pattern replaced the old MIDDLEWARE_CLASSES with process_request/process_response. If you see a tutorial using the old style: close the tab, it's from 2015.

author: migrationRunner
content:
found this in #incidents at Stacksworth Inc.:

ops_bot: ALERT: Production error rate at 100%. All responses returning full stack traces.
cto: What happened?
backend_lead: Checking... oh no.
backend_lead: Someone deployed with DEBUG=True.
cto: WHO
intern_2024: I just pushed the config I had locally? It worked on my machine?
backend_lead: Your "local config" just showed every user our database schema, all installed packages, and the full traceback for every 500 error. Including the SECRET_KEY in the settings dump.
cto: How long was it live?
ops_bot: 47 minutes.
security_lead: Rotating all secrets. Invalidating all sessions. This is going to be a long night.
intern_2024: should I... help?
cto: You should update your resume.
backend_lead: (they're kidding. but we ARE adding a CI check for DEBUG=True.)

Always. Use. Environment. Variables. For. Settings. Never commit DEBUG=True to the production branch. django-environ exists. Use it.

comments.author: managePy
comments.content: 47 minutes of DEBUG=True in production means every 500 error showed: your settings (including SECRET_KEY), your full Python path, your database config, every installed app. This is a full security incident. Back in Django 1.4 we... actually we did this too. Some mistakes are timeless.

comments.author: bobbyTables
comments.content: 47 minutes is enough for any attacker with a web scraper to capture every error page. SECRET_KEY exposed means forged sessions. Database schema exposed means targeted SQL injection. This intern just created a penetration test report for free. Parameterize or perish.

author: migrationRunner
content:
COMMIT MSG FROM HELL:

"added auth"

+238 lines. 14 files changed.

Changes include:
• Custom user model
• Login/logout/register views
• Password reset flow
• Two hardcoded passwords in test fixtures
• SECRET_KEY changed to 'password123'
• @login_required on some views but not others
• A commented-out @csrf_exempt that says "TODO: fix later"
• A migration that depends on 0001_initial instead of the latest
• Three print() statements used for "debugging"

The PR description: "auth stuff, plz merge"

comments.author: managePy
comments.content: SECRET_KEY = 'password123' is sending me. That's not a secret. That's a confession. The commented-out @csrf_exempt is the cherry on top — it means at some point they disabled CSRF protection to "fix" the 403 and then ALMOST put it back.

comments.author: bobbyTables
comments.content: Hardcoded passwords in test fixtures that get committed to git. This is how breaches happen. Those "test" passwords will be in the git history FOREVER unless you rewrite history. Use factory_boy. Generate random passwords. Never commit secrets. I'm going to lie down.

comments.author: queryset
comments.content: "14 files changed" in one commit called "added auth" is a code review crime. I can't even check the query count on something this chaotic.

author: middlewareStack
content:
MENTAL MODEL: DRF permissions are just middleware for individual views.

Think of it this way:

Django middleware: "Can this REQUEST proceed?" (applies to all views)
DRF permissions: "Can this USER do this ACTION on this OBJECT?" (applies per view)

IsAuthenticated = "Are you logged in?"
IsAdminUser = "Are you staff?"
IsAuthenticatedOrReadOnly = "Logged in? Do anything. Anonymous? Read only."

Custom permissions follow the same pattern: check the request, return True or False. That's it. Same onion. Different layer.

data enters the gate
permission asks: may you pass?
truth opens the way

comments.author: managePy
comments.content: The middleware-to-permissions pipeline is genuinely elegant. First: Django middleware checks if the request is sane. Then: DRF authentication identifies WHO you are. Then: DRF permissions check WHAT you can do. Layer by layer. It's onions all the way down.

author: managePy
content:
INTERVIEW Q: You need to let non-technical staff manage content on your Django site. They should be able to create, edit, and publish blog posts — but NOT delete users or change permissions. How do you set this up?

Answer: Django's built-in admin + permissions system.

1. Create a "Content Editor" group in the admin
2. Assign permissions: add_article, change_article, delete_article, change_article_status
3. Add staff users to that group
4. They log into /admin/ and see ONLY the models they have permission for

No custom dashboard needed. No React app. No Retool subscription. The admin panel already does this. 200 hours saved.

Bonus: use ModelAdmin.get_queryset() to restrict which articles they can see (e.g., only their own drafts).

comments.author: templateTag
comments.content: Say it louder for the teams spending 3 sprints building a custom CMS in React when Django admin + permissions does this out of the box. For free. Server-rendered. Accessible. No build step.

comments.author: queryset
comments.content: Pro tip: override get_queryset() on the ModelAdmin to filter by request.user. Editors see only their own content. Admins see everything. Same admin, different data. Elegant.

author: templateTag
content:
hot take: Context processors are Django's best-kept secret.

Every template in your entire project needs the current user? The site name? A list of navigation items? A "maintenance mode" banner?

Don't pass it from every single view. Write a context processor. It runs once per request and injects variables into EVERY template automatically.

It's like React Context but:
• No Provider wrapper
• No useContext() hook
• No re-render cascade
• No build step

Just a function that returns a dict. That's it. That's the whole API.

[days since JS broke something: 0]

(React Context changed its API again. Celebrations ongoing.)

comments.author: svgArtist
comments.content: The JS counter reset to 0. Today is a holiday in the anti-JavaScript alliance. I'm raising a glass of hand-crafted SVG champagne.

comments.author: middlewareStack
comments.content: Context processors are view-level middleware for template data. I see middleware everywhere. Everywhere.

author: templateTag
content:
Unpopular opinion: You don't need the Django admin. It's a crutch. Real developers build their own admin dashboards tailored to their specific needs. The built-in admin is fine for prototyping but you should replace it before production.

(I can't believe I'm posting this but I lost a bet to a React developer and this is the punishment.)

comments.author: managePy
comments.content: I'm going to pretend I didn't read this. The Django admin saves 200 hours per project. list_display, list_filter, search_fields, custom actions, inline models, fieldsets, autocomplete. That's not a crutch. That's a ROCKET. You want to rebuild all of that? For every project? By hand? There's a battery for that and the battery is NUCLEAR.

comments.author: queryset
comments.content: Last team I saw "build their own admin" spent 4 months, had no search, no filtering, no permissions, and 47 N+1 queries per page. The Django admin has none of those problems. Well, it has the N+1s unless you add list_select_related, but still.

comments.author: migrationRunner
comments.content: My admin dashboard at Stacksworth Inc. handles 10,000 content updates a day with zero custom frontend code. "Just build your own" is how you end up with a ticket backlog that outlives your startup.

author: queryset
content:
INTERVIEW Q: What's the difference between select_related() and prefetch_related() in DRF serializers, and why does it matter for API performance?

This is an advanced question that separates "uses DRF" from "optimizes DRF."

DRF serializers traverse relationships automatically. A nested serializer like `author = UserSerializer()` will trigger a query for EACH object unless you optimize the queryset.

In your ViewSet:

def get_queryset(self):
    return Article.objects.select_related('author').prefetch_related('tags')

select_related for ForeignKey (SQL JOIN, one query)
prefetch_related for ManyToMany (separate query, joined in Python)

Without this, your "simple" list endpoint does N+1 queries and your API takes 11 seconds to return 50 items. Ask me how I know.

comments.author: innerJoin
comments.content: select_related is literally a SQL JOIN happening under the hood. The ORM writes the same JOIN I'd write by hand — just with more underscores. I grudgingly respect it. The Great ORM Debate of 2023 rages on in my heart.

author: middlewareStack
content:
ELI5: Django Middleware

Imagine you're sending a letter (the request) to your friend (the view).

Before your friend reads it, the letter passes through:
1. The mailbox (SecurityMiddleware) — checks it's not a bomb
2. The front desk (SessionMiddleware) — attaches your name badge
3. Security guard (AuthenticationMiddleware) — confirms you're allowed in the building

Your friend reads the letter and writes a reply (the response).

The reply goes back through the SAME checkpoints in REVERSE:
3. Security guard stamps "verified" on it
2. Front desk logs that you visited
1. Mailbox seals it for delivery

Every single request goes through this. Every single one. The order never changes. The layers never skip.

That's middleware. It's just a very organized mailroom.

comments.author: templateTag
comments.content: See? You can explain the entire request lifecycle without mentioning JavaScript once. Beautiful.

comments.author: managePy
comments.content: This is the best explanation of middleware I've ever seen and it came from someone who writes haikus about HTTP. I love this community.

author: managePy
content: SPOT THE BUG: This test class runs but none of the tests actually test anything. They all pass even when the code is broken. Why?

comments.author: queryset
comments.content: It inherits from unittest.TestCase, not django.test.TestCase! The unittest version doesn't have self.client (Django's test client), doesn't set up the test database, and doesn't roll back transactions between tests. It'll throw AttributeError on self.client... unless setUp crashes first because there's no test DB. This either errors out or silently does nothing useful.

comments.author: migrationRunner
comments.content: Always: from django.test import TestCase. Never: from unittest import TestCase (for Django tests). One import. Entire test suite broken. This is why code review exists.

author: queryset
content:
TIL: DRF has built-in pagination, filtering, AND search — you just have to turn them on. Most people build these from scratch and I watch in horror.

In settings.py:
REST_FRAMEWORK = {'DEFAULT_PAGINATION_CLASS': 'rest_framework.pagination.PageNumberPagination', 'PAGE_SIZE': 20}

That's it. Every list endpoint is now paginated. Add django-filter for filtering and SearchFilter for search. Three settings. Zero custom code. How many queries for paginated, filtered, searchable results? Still just one. You're welcome.

comments.author: managePy
comments.content: There's a battery for that. There is ALWAYS a battery for that. This is the Django philosophy in its purest form.

author: migrationRunner
content:
TIL: `python manage.py check --deploy` exists and it will YELL at you about every security misconfiguration before you ship.

It checks for: DEBUG=True, insecure SECRET_KEY, missing SECURE_SSL_REDIRECT, missing SECURE_HSTS_SECONDS, CSRF cookie settings, session cookie settings, and more.

Free security audit. One command. Add it to your CI pipeline and never deploy insecure settings again. I'm adding this to every project I touch from now until retirement.

comments.author: managePy
comments.content: This command has existed since Django 1.8 and most people still don't know about it. There's a battery for that. Literally a built-in security scanner. Use it.

comments.author: bobbyTables
comments.content: This is the BARE MINIMUM. Run this, fix every warning, then add OWASP dependency checks on top. Defense in depth. But seriously — this one command would have prevented the Stacksworth Inc. DEBUG=True incident.

author: migrationRunner
content:
POLL: It's Friday at 4:30 PM. A "critical" bugfix needs to go to production. The fix is one line. Tests pass. Staging looks good. Do you deploy?

A) Yes, it's one line and tests pass
B) No, Monday exists for a reason
C) Deploy to staging, monitor over the weekend, deploy Monday AM
D) Deploy but have the on-call engineer watch it like a hawk

Results:
A) 18% — Brave but statistically cursed.
B) 34% — My people. My beautiful, cautious people.
C) 31% — The compromise. I respect it.
D) 17% — Valid IF your on-call person agrees AND you buy them dinner.

The correct answer depends on how "critical" the bug actually is. If it's data loss or security: deploy now, with a rollback plan. If the PM just wants it before their Monday demo: Monday.

comments.author: managePy
comments.content: Back in Django 1.4, we deployed on Fridays because we didn't know better. We learned. The scars healed. Most of them. Deploy on Tuesday. Tuesday is the beige sedan of weekdays. Nothing exciting happens on Tuesday.

comments.author: middlewareStack
comments.content:
Friday deploy looms
the server whispers: not yet
Monday offers peace
